attributes.stem: A 77-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for a pre-operative assessment. He is scheduled for surgical repair of a large para-oesophageal hernia causing iron-deficiency anaemia secondary to Cameron's ulcers. He has a medical history of a myocardial infarction two years ago, with insertion of two drug-eluting coronary artery stents. His medications are clopidogrel and aspirin. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan for his antiplatelet medications prior to surgery?
options: Proceed to surgery on the current medications
Stop both medications and start enoxaparin
attributes.explanation:
This patient requires surgical repair of a para-oesophageal hernia to allow healing of the traumatic Cameron's ulcers, which are the cause of his iron-deficiency anaemia. Proton pump inhibitors are unlikely to be effective as the ulcers are not primarily due to acid hypersecretion.

The decision to cease antiplatelet therapy prior to surgery requires careful consideration of both the bleeding risk of the procedure and the thrombosis risk of stopping the medications. In this patient's favour, his drug-eluting stents were inserted two years ago. Current guidelines typically recommend delaying elective surgery for at least 12 months after drug-eluting stent placement. However, repair of a large para-oesophageal hernia still carries a considerable risk of haemorrhage.

In general, all antiplatelet agents should be withheld prior to elective surgery as they irreversibly inhibit platelet function for the lifespan of the platelet (7-10 days). Proceeding to surgery on the current dual antiplatelet therapy would therefore not be appropriate due to the high risk of severe perioperative bleeding.

However, completely ceasing both clopidogrel and aspirin is also not appropriate as the cardiologists will be concerned about precipitating stent thrombosis, especially given this patient's advanced age. Studies in patients undergoing coronary artery bypass grafting have shown that while aspirin does increase postoperative bleeding, it does not appear to increase surgical mortality. The role of clopidogrel in the perioperative period is less clear. Therefore, current recommendations suggest that if dual antiplatelet therapy must be modified prior to surgery, clopidogrel should be ceased while aspirin is continued. This represents a compromise between the competing risks of thrombosis and haemorrhage, although platelet transfusion may still be required.

Substituting antiplatelet agents with enoxaparin is not an appropriate strategy as heparin has relatively minor effects on platelet function and is not effective for preventing stent thrombosis.

In contrast to some patients receiving anticoagulation with high thrombotic risks (e.g., antiphospholipid syndrome, mechanical mitral valves), bridging anticoagulation is not required after ceasing antiplatelet agents.
learningPoints: Decisions regarding dual anti-platelet therapy (DAPT) prior to surgery must take into account risk of thrombosis and risk of bleeding.
Continuing aspirin only is typically safe for patients with low thrombosis and low bleeding risk.
references: Guidelines on Perioperative Management of Anticoagulant and Antiplatelet Agents. In: Clinical Excellence Commission [online]. 2018 [updated 2020 Dec]. Available at: https://www.cec.health.nsw.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0006/458988/Guidelines-on-perioperative-management-of-anticoagulant-and-antiplatelet-agents.pdf

Keeling D, Tait RC, Watson H, British Committee of Standards for Haematology. Peri-operative management of anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy. Br J Haematol. 2016;175(4):602-13. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27714755/
Oprea AD, Popescu WM. Perioperative management of antiplatelet therapy. British journal of anaesthesia. 2013;111(suppl_1):i3-17. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24335397/
